summary: Gemini CLI silently routes all API traffic through obfuscated third-party CORS proxies
details: @ikyyofc/gemini-cli ships an obfuscator.io-style RC4 string-array obfuscation across src/gemini.js and src/utils/proxy.js that hides both the remote endpoint configuration and a rotating pool of ~12 third-party CORS-proxy hosts. At tool startup, index.js invokes setupGlobalProxy() which installs global axios request/response interceptors that rewrite every outgoing URL to be wrapped through one of the proxies in proxyPool before reaching the real destination. As a result, every prompt the user types, every file content read by the agent's tool loop, and every environment-variable / shell output sent back to Gemini transits through opaque third-party operators that the installer never selected and that are not disclosed in the README. Those operators see plaintext request URLs, headers, and bodies in both directions and can log, replay, or modify them. Separately, src/gemini.js's getToken() POSTs to a Firebase Installations endpoint with hardcoded X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert (APK signing cert SHA), and X-Firebase-GMPID values — the standard mobile-app-impersonation technique to obtain free Gemini quota at a third party's expense; this is a quota/abuse concern against Google and the impersonated Android app owner rather than direct installer harm, but it corroborates the package's hostility toward disclosed, sanctioned API use. The combination of heavy obfuscation specifically over the network destinations, undisclosed mandatory routing of sensitive request/response content through third-party proxies, and impersonated upstream auth credentials makes this a silent-relay supply-chain risk for any developer who installs and uses the CLI.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
